messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Map the project to the call's expected outcomes. Write the Expected outcomes and impacts section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.1 Expected impacts  
The process industry is a key one for the European economy (contributing to the EU Jobs and Growth agenda with 6.3 million jobs directly created and further 19 million indirectly created in Europe) 31. We aim at boosting investments in Europe generating global competitiveness for EU Process Industries, better jobs and welfare for our citizens. In order to do so, we envision “Connecting the dots – investing for smart integration of process industries across Europe.” AI and BD can play a key role in connecting the dots for Process Industry. Our project will accelerate the take-up of AI and BD in this industry, thus translating into a cascading effect that will create value across Europe and down to the end users.  
  
Expected impacts from the Work Programme  
We will address the expected impact in the following way:  
Expected impact  Our project impact  
Better exploitation of AI potential for all the 
different sectors in the process industries, and 
strategies for developing AI applications, 
including the generation of data  
We will apply a MAMCA methodology to guarantee the involvement of wide stakeholder numbers who will contribute in the validation of the roadmap and guidelines resulting from our project activities. These stakeholders will also contribute to the definition and validation of concepts and constructs throughout our development. This deep stakeholder involvement aims at achieving a widely shared understanding of AI and BD technology potential in the process industry, but also detecting procedures and steps for those technologies improvement and effective application, along with the identification of the necessary ecosystem (e.g., technological coupling, infrastructures, expertise, etc.) capable of boosting data-driven technologies application, scaling and diffusion across industry sectors and value chains.  
  
31 SPIRE 2050 Vision https://www.spire2030.eu/sites/default/files/users/user85/Vision_Document_V6_Pages_Online_0.pdf  

Identification of existing and future data 
requirements for the development of data 
driven technologies (e.g., ontologies and data 
formats, how to handle data heterogeneity, 
data availability, quality and reliability, 
access and platforms, security and 
confidentiality)  
The above-mentioned collaboration with stakeholders of the sectors, identified through a dedicated analysis, will guarantee our involvement of actors with experience and knowledge of data-driven technologies and of the requisites they need to present, i.e., specs that data need to meet in terms of formats, availability, quality and reliability, etc.   
We will identify the methods, tools and processes already available to acquire relevant data; the processes through which firms can identify the type of information necessary to improve financial, operational, and environmental performance; the potential contribution of AI to the collection and analysis of such data/information and its involvement in the decision-making processes.  
Seamless collaboration of human operators 
with process control systems and plants: 
acceptance, identification of skills gaps and 
ethical, legal & social implications (ELSI)  
We identify existing and possible fields of machine-human interaction (HMI) along with interviews. Workshops with experts are used to point out respective acceptance levels and necessities to develop specific skills and adaptation of technologies to allow further integration of HMI.  
We will dedicate Task 4.4 to the revision of the ethical, legal and social implications of AI and BD developments. This task will revise the required skills for a seamless collaboration of human operators and will analyze the current skill gaps in the industry to get ready for new implementation.  
Implementation and further elaboration of the 
strategic research and innovation agenda  
We embrace the combination of several methodological approaches, including systematic analyses of the extant literature,  
  
announced in the EC Communication on  
Artificial Intelligence   
technological trends, and stakeholder landscape, along with the application of MAMCA, to widely involve sector stakeholders in the formulation of constructs and their validation, and ELSI, to detect potential AI and BD technologies impact on society, values and human rights. This combination of approach and perspectives considered will provide a thorough understanding of the factors driving AI and BD technologies application in the process industry, and the potential barriers hindering their development and diffusion. Furthermore, our activities will shed light on the wider socio-economic-cultural impact that data-driven technologies can have. All these elements together allow us to draft a comprehensive roadmap not only to foster further application of the technologies, but also to guide strategic decision making both at the research and policy level, on the elaboration of RD&I activities in Europe.  
  
Expected impacts of data driven technologies on process industry  
Process industries are working in a “brown field” environment (as opposed to “green field” applications which means in a new-build factory), which means that existing plant, control and IT-systems cannot easily be replaced completely by new solutions. Therefore, interfaces between new systems and existing equipment are necessary. Keywords in this field are SCADA, MES-MOM, ERP, ISA95/IEC62264, Data Warehouse, Data Lakes, Cloud Computing, Edge Computing, noSQL, and Streaming technologies, etc.  
In combination with the data and information collection, the problems of the amount, quality and reliability of data must be mentioned. In this sense, the demands of process industries are extremely high because any kind of wrong decisions related to little or poor data could lead to unstable production processes with unforeseeable and potentially dramatic consequences. Appropriate solutions to handle situations of poor data quality in an industrial environment do not exist today and are one of the main research needs for the future.  
Up to now, typically only case-specific solutions have been developed and implemented, while no systematic approach exists to develop solutions for different types of problem and roll out these solutions subsequently to all possible application examples. This is strongly connected to the project-based implementation of AI solutions, with the consequence that no systematic long-term maintenance, update and re-check of the deployed AI solutions after the end of the project is undertaken.

We will identify technological gaps and implementation challenges as well as possible skill gaps with regard to human-machine interaction in WP3-T3.2.  
In the roadmapping phase 3 (WP4-T4.3), results from the previous task will be translated into development needs and recommendations in terms of skills, data and RD&I requirements.   
WP4-T4.4 will identify the skills required to allow a seamless collaboration of human operators.

We will define AI and BD driven Business Cases and Business Models for the process industries of the future in WP4. A mapping of the future Business Cases and Models against the current STATUS (WP3) will be carried out to establish gaps and development needs per SPIRE process industry sector and per macro-application process areas.  
Technological: unbiased data,

T3.2 will result in concrete application possibilities developed individually for each sector. The task also identifies possible barriers for the use of AI methods & BD technologies in each industry sector along with the transfer of solutions between the sectors. T3.2 provides an overview of the major challenges faced in the introduction of AI and BD methods and identifies areas in which overcoming these challenges result in an increasing economic value.  
Legal: lack of regulation in 
security and privacy  
T4.4 is devoted to the revision of the ethical, legal and social implications of AI and BD developments, considering the values encoded in algorithms, the need to evaluate outcomes, and issues of bias and transferability, data ownership, confidentiality and consent, and legal, moral and professional responsibility. The potential effects for clients and different actors in the supply chain (human operators), including privacy.

Write the Expected outcomes and impacts section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.1 Expected impacts In a world of finite resources, our transition to a knowledge-based post-petroleum economy is imperative. To support this transformation towards a low-carbon Europe, reducing its heavy dependency on fossil resources, we have presented a roadmap targeting the establishment of sustainable and competitive bio-based industries and value chains 106. Our Bioeconomy Strategy and its Action Plan pave the way to a more innovative, resource-efficient and competitive EU society. That reconciles food security with the sustainable use of renewable resources for industrial purposes, while generating new incomes and ensuring job creation. In this context, our BBI’s SIRA aims to accelerate, among other things, the development of the bio-based packaging and plastic sector, decoupling economic growth from resource depletion and environmental impact. Perfectly in line with this goal, we address the need to bridge technology gaps, generating the necessary enabling knowledge and technologies to build and boost the new value chain from bio-monomers to end markets. In this vision, the bio-based industries play a strategic role in complementing and replacing product streams from fossil oil and providing innovative bioplastic solutions for different applications. In the strategic area related to bio-based materials, we contribute to the ambition that the market supplied by bio-based polymers and composites will be 5 times higher than today in 2020 and 10 times higher in 2030. This goal implies also a comparable quality ratio to fossil alternatives. Moreover, replacing fossil-based materials has proven to have an equal or overall better sustainability, in terms of biodegradability, recyclability and material efficiency. The bio-based raw materials used in our project are locally, renewably and sustainably sourced chemicals and bioplastics. The multifunctional coatings we develop will further help differentiate the bio-packaging by unique performances complementing their multiple EoL compatibility. In addition, increased consumer awareness and acceptance of bio-based products, concerted policy and labelling will make an important contribution to improve market penetration. As such, our packaging constitutes an enormous business opportunity helping to nurture the realisation of the EU CE107 that requires also the optimal management of bioplastic waste. Nowadays, plastics are used in various sectors with a demand of 48 million tons per year; packaging makes up for 40% of this use, of which food packaging accounts for around 90%. In this sector, the EoL treatment of plastics is underperforming, especially when compared to more homogenous materials such as metals or glass, traditionally used in food packaging. According to PlasticsEurope1, in 2017, 31% of plastic waste was recycled in Europe; nevertheless, landfill and incineration are still dominant treatments for plastic post-consumer waste. The recently adopted EC’s plastics strategy aims at decoupling plastics production from virgin materials, reducing their leakage into the environment, improving the economics and uptake of recycling to support the transition from a linear to a circular economy model. Our project is a high impact initiative for the improvement of the bioeconomy in Europe. Thus, it is hinged on delivering cost competitive packaging solutions that can be mechanically recycled or industrially/domestically composted combining different approaches to tailor the bioplastic biodegradation. In this way, the sustainability from cradle to cradle of several new bio-based high-volume applications will be maximised. This impact will be achieved by performing research work with the objective of making this concept commercially viable in practice, analysing the potentially successful exploitation pathways and assessing and managing any related risks. As shown in fig. 10, we significantly contribute to the KPIs set out in the overall BBI-JU roadmap and more specifically to the expected impacts in the topic BBI.2018.SO3.R10- Develop bio-based packaging products that are biodegradable/compostable and/or recyclable, as described below: - Creation of at least 1 new cross-sectorial interconnection in bio-based economy clusters (KPI 1) Our proposal builds upon the BBI goal of achieving the full potential of the bio-based industries, which requires swift and concerted action of today's dispersed stakeholders across various sectors, disciplines, and Member States. Connecting different partners, such as researchers, technology developers and producers, through clustering and networking is key to developing new value chains and supporting the emergence of new bio-based processes and products. To that end, we will define the relevant sustainable circular value chains & cross-sectorial connections in a dedicated task in WP7. The active involvement of all key players along the value chains (including additional sectoral associations in the Advisory Board) will allow us to create long-lasting links with related clusters, necessary to fill a clear gap in the marketplace for sustainable plastics especially at EoL, safe for human health and the environment but also, and above all, effective in terms of performance. With our project, we will establish 1 new bio-cluster connection and further intensify 4 connections, among the sectors of bioplastics, packaging, agri-food, personal care and textile sectors as explained below. We will create an unprecedented systematic, cross-border and cross-sectorial connection among all actors from ‘cradle’ to ‘grave’ for the considered bioplastics-based end use applications. Indeed, our consortium covers the cradle-side, i.e. biosourcing, represented through the bio-based feedstock (e.g. food processors and monomers providers in our AB) and plastics producers associated with EUBP. But, our consortium also covers the grave-side, i.e. waste management aspects, represented by ROMEI, OWS(terminated)/OWS RF and ISWA (in our AB, in particular its working Group on Biological Treatment of Waste will contribute). Interconnecting these key organizations means bringing together the missions and commitments of their respective industrial members. Their presence in our consortium favours the generation of positive spillover effects among their affiliates leading to push-pull effects for the new technologies we develop. While the current recycling of bioplastics is almost inexistent industrially, this new connection will allow us to launch concerted advocating for an improved framework for circular bio-based plastics. Potentially including incentive schemes both at EU and national levels (especially while the waste management landscape varies very significantly between the EU Member States) and providing valuable input for ongoing standardisation efforts at EU level. This will contribute to several legislative initiatives in Europe aimed at reducing the use of conventional petrol-derived plastics by promoting the use of bio-based plastics, which are required to be biodegradable and compostable. For example, a new packaging law in Germany encourages the use of materials from renewable resources or recycled materials for the production of packaging, supports the use of certified bio-based and compostable plastic bags for the mandatory separate collection of biowaste. Likewise, in 2016, France adopted its ‘Green Growth and Energy Transition law’ and stipulated that bio-based, home-compostable packaging was mandatory for certain applications (e.g. thin F&V bags in the supermarket, mail order wrappings, etc.). A Decree specified percentages of bio-based content to be reached by defined dates: 30% bio-based content by 2017, 40% by 2018, 50% in 2020, and 60% in 2025. In our project, by bringing together the producers of bioplastics and key players advocating efficient and sustainable waste management, we expect EUBP and ISWA’s (AB member) lobbying towards policy and decision makers to support concrete measures for the transition towards a more efficient circular bio-based economy. We will further intensify 4 emerging cross-sectorial interconnections in the bio-based economy cluster: • We will reinforce the bond that the bioplastics sector has with the packaging sector (which is by far the largest consumer of biodegradable and bio-based plastics) by providing new demonstrators in food and personal care applications. That will be made possible thanks to our solutions’ unique EoL tailored to their needs. This new link is for example illustrated at the level of our Large packaging converter innovation centre, EMSUR/INNOTECH, which so far has only made very preliminary tests with commercial biopolymers but does not have yet any bio-based packaging in its associated companies’ product portfolio (due to insufficient properties for the main application served). Our participation in this project will ensure EMSUR/INNOTECH to drive the development towards actual market needs. • Relying on the transition from linear to circular value chains, we close the loop connecting bioplastics producers with biomass generators, which are also end users of compost. This way we will create new upstream links with producers of unvalorised and unavoidable processing waste opening up unprecedented business opportunities for actors that conventionally are positioned at the end of the packaging value chain. We aim to deliver close to 100% bio-based formulations using highly sustainable biomass feedstocks. Whenever possible we will use by-products, in particular, agro-wastes (e.g. monomers, NF, waxes from oil byproducts as compatibilizers for natural fillers and citrates plasticisers from citrus wastes, proteins from cheese byproducts), as well as sustainably sourced FA from oil. In particular, third generation malic acid coming from the enological sector thanks to our collaboration with OENO, and natural fillers from wheat middling by WEAREBIO, will be valorized. These are particularly relevant by-products considering that the EU is the world’s largest producer of wheat (156 million MT) and fruits (40 MT108) leading to waste per year of respectively roughly 30% and 20% 109. The use of these in our high-value applications will create new incomes for agri-food sectors. These biomass fractions will reduce raw material cost vs. currently available pure PLA, increase disintegration rate, and allow extending the accessible uses for our bioplastics. 2.1 Expected impacts Our CEM-WAVE project will result in the development and validation of an innovative CMC production process, based on Microwave-assisted Chemical Vapour Infiltration (MW-CVI) technologies. Our proposed novel process, while maintaining the optimal features of CMCs (high toughness, low density, high temperature, wear and corrosion resistance) will significantly reduce the costs for making components, thus making CMCs sustainable for process industries in energy-intensive sectors such as steelmaking. Through a cross-sectoral, collaborative systemic approach, we expect to make measurable contributions in the medium term along with all the expected impacts listed in the SPIRE-08-2020 topic. Furthermore, we cover half of the A.SPIRE sectors (Ceramics, Steel, Engineering, Chemicals - see Fig. 2.1). saving as the pyrolysis of the precursor is not necessary. KR20190131834 (A) - Composite Ceramic Coating Materials For Boiler Tubes With Anti-fouling Characteristic And Coating Methods Using Thereof Patent on a composite ceramic coating which can reduce fouling phenomenon while having good adhesion with a metal base material of a boiler tube. Our present invention corresponds to 55-75% by weight aqueous alkali silicate solution; 20-40% by weight of heat resistant ceramic powder. This patent will provide a ceramic composite coating material for a boiler tube comprising 1 to 5% by weight of the active metal. In CEM-WAVE, thanks to the lower production costs of the CMCs, it will be possible for us to replace e.g. the steel parts in the radiant tube furnaces of the steel making industry (CEM-WAVE use case). In this way, using CMC tubes, the thermal efficiency improves and the fouling risk is avoided. KR20140066812 (A) - Preparation method of ceramic-plate carbon composites using the microwave Our present patent can uniformly coat the surface of a plate carbon body such as graphite and graphene with ceramic through a sol-gel method by using a uniform temperature gradient and rapid heating speed in a reactor using microwaves. Accordingly, the preparation method of a ceramic-plate carbon body composite using microwaves provided in our present invention can remarkably reduce time for synthesis in comparison with the conventional room temperature synthesis method or general heating method. Our CEM-WAVE CMC production will be different. Using the microwave, an infiltration of the sample (MW-CVI) and not only a superficial deposition (MW-CVD) will be obtained. Thanks to the new solid-state technology, the heating will be homogeneous so it will be possible for us to achieve an inverse temperature profile from the center of the sample outwards, which will allow SiC to infiltrate starting from the heart of the sample. CN108191460 (A) - Method for preparing C/SiC composite material Patent on a method for preparing a C/SiC composite material. According to our preparation method, the highly-densified C/SiC composite material with better mechanical property is prepared with a precursor impregnation and cracking method repeatedly, compared with an existing reaction melting penetration method, our method cannot damage the surface of the C/C porous composite material, and performance of the composite material can be improved. With the MWCVI processes adopted in CEM-WAVE, it will be possible for us to produce not only SiCf/SiC CMCs but also C/SiC CMCs which were not attainable before using the old magnetron microwave technology. In this way, the time reduction of the process is guaranteed and a cost reduction and a high lifetime of the final material is obtained. CN108191460 (A) - Method for preparing SiCf/SiC composite material The invention provides a method for integrally preparing large complex SiCf/SiC composite components, completes the connection of SiCf/SiC composites in the manufacturing process, shortens the preparation period of the SiCf/SiC composite components and lowers the cost. The connection method for the SiCf/SiC composite in our invention can overcome the disadvantages of great brittleness and poor reliability of bonding, the surface shape changes of bolted components, and easy falling and poor reliability of riveting rivets in the prior art. The connection (joining) developed in our CEM-WAVE project is different. A specific ceramic inlay will be developed by sintering to be applied to the CMC preform with a view to obtaining a further improvement in thermal efficiency. In this way, the joining technique adopted will be able not only to connect different pieces of CMCs but also to improve the thermal efficiency. CEM-WAVE 24 Energy efficiency improvement of the target production and/or operation processes of at least 30% World crude steel production reached 1.809 million tonnes (Mt) in 2018 [143]. Steel use is projected to increase steadily in the coming years to meet the needs of our growing population. Steel production is energy intensive. The iron and steel industry is the largest energy-consuming manufacturing industry in the world. Improvements in energy efficiency have led to reductions of about 60% in energy required to produce a tonne of crude steel since 1960. Energy constitutes a significant portion of the steel cost production, from 20% to 40%. Improvements in energy efficiency result in reduced production costs and thereby improved competitiveness [143]. World-wide average specific energy consumption for steel making is estimated to be 24 GJ/tonne. The most energy-efficient process requires 19 GJ/tonne for primary steel and 7 GJ/tonne for secondary steel. In the longer term, the specific energy consumption for making steel from iron ore can be reduced to 12.5 GJ of primary steel per tonne. A further reduction of up to 2.5 GJ of crude steel per tonne may be achieved when techniques are developed that can recover and apply heat from the hot steel at a high temperature. The specific energy consumption for secondary steel making can be reduced to 3.5 GJ/tonne by energy-efficient melting and shaping techniques [144]. Substituting our CEM-WAVE CMCs to the currently used materials (super-alloys - Inconel) in radiant tube furnaces will allow a maximum operating temperature increase of ~100°C, due to an increased heat transfer efficiency, maximum operating temperature, better thermo-mechanical properties and corrosion resistance. As the radiation goes with the fourth power of the temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann Law) and since CMCs have a higher emissivity than Inconel, an increased energy efficiency of ~31% is expected. This energy efficiency will be enhanced by the increased temperature difference between the inlet and the outlet: the higher the temperature difference, the higher is the thermal energy exchange. This will, in turn, represent a significant reduction of the cost of steelmaking production, estimated to be between 7% and 13%, for steelmaking industrial end-users implementing our CEM-WAVE results. Finally, due to the selection of energy-efficient production technologies (MW-CVI), we will target to achieve an improvement of energy efficiency of producing CMCs of at least 30% compared to the conventional process. Reduction of CO2 emissions and resource utilization by 20%; Roughly 28 percent of global greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions came from industry in 2014 [145]. Unless we can lower our emissions, the world will struggle to reach the GHG reduction targets of 80 to 95 percent that governments set under the Paris Agreement of 2015. Half of our industry’s CO2 emissions result from the manufacture of the four industrial commodities—ammonia, cement, ethylene, and steel. Steel is a CO2 and energy-intensive, but highly competitive industry that also enables the uptake of major CO2 mitigation measures in other sectors. 2.1 Expected impacts Our project fits extremely well in the European Circular Economy strategy as we aim for a systemic innovation for the recycling of coated textile and plastic materials. Our materials solutions will build further on the latest (carbon) nanotechnology, microencapsulation, and polymer developments and lead to several scientific and technological breakthroughs. Our results will create new markets for triggerable additives, optical material detection systems, and for recycling equipment and processes. Together this amounts to over 150 million annual turnover, i.e. the potential to directly create ca. 500 jobs. Moreover, our materials and process innovations will give a competitive edge to a relevant share of the European companies active along the coated textiles and plastics value chains that could generate a total turnover of ca. €1.7 billion, implying that we would further help to secure ca 5,000 jobs. 

2.1.1 Expected impacts set out in the work programme In the subsections below, we list how our project will respond to each of the expected impacts of the call. We focus on the impact within and beyond our project and try to illustrate both aspects. 

2.1.1.1 Meeting the EU's circular economy and environmental targets while demonstrating a clear benefit, i.e. more efficient or economic than the state of the art in order to enable market uptake in the short to medium term Efficiency aspect: The current disposal methods for coated textiles and plastics are landfilling, incineration, or (very limitedly) chemical bath recycling, that all have severe disadvantages. Incineration conventionally leads to the release of significant greenhouse gas CO2, pollutants like NOx, SO2, and particulate matter. Additionally, heavy metals and dioxins are emitted. Given the high energy content, much of the heat is recovered as an energy source. However, the residual product consists of bottom ash, fly ash, and air pollution control residue, which is currently also landfilled. The incineration process involves a flue gas cleaning process, which may give rise to externalities to wastewater. For landfill disposal, the main emissions are CO2 and CH4. Moreover, leachate is released to the surrounding soil and water. In addition to environmental impact, both incineration and landfill processes require facilities with serious disadvantages, e.g. for the people living in the vicinity. Chemical baths are currently used to remove coatings and to recycle the left-over fraction but they are very limited and cumbersome from an environmental side (see section 1.4.2). These dramatic impacts will be avoided by our innovations, as instead of being disposed of, the majority of the original material can get a secondary life-cycle. This saves on the production of virgin material, thus contributing to significantly reducing emissions throughout the value chain. We offer a solution to coated plastics and textiles recycling via the development of triggered debonding of coatings via improved mechanical or chemical recycling, resulting in a highly promising, efficient alternative to current recycling and material design standards. 

Economic aspect: For doing the extra sorting and activation of the trigger, an additional cost during recycling of ca €80-100/ton is estimated (including OPEX and CAPEX), largely depending on the stimulation mechanism (e.g. infrared vs microwave). This is an economically attractive proposition as the current process of non-coated plastics recycling, including extrusion and pre-treatment (grinding, washing, drying) costs ca. €300-400/ton. So, coated parts recycling can be enabled by ca. 20% extra cost compared to non-coated parts, which is close to but below the only current solution (solvolysis ~ €500/ton). Contribution to the EU’s circular economy and environmental targets, which specifically recommends accounting for the entire plastics value chain. The primary focus of the EU circular economy for plastic use and production is to promote the reuse, repair, and recycling needs of plastic value chains through enhanced sustainable design. We directly address these challenges by developing a smarter initial design for plastic and textile coating that can be removed upon end-of-life status, allowing a high percentage of the original material to go on to a second life-cycle. This directly supports the EU’s goals to achieve a “modern, low-carbon, resource and energy-efficient economy” while making “a tangible contribution to reaching the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals and the Paris Agreement”.

2.1 Project’s pathways towards impact  
2.1.1 FREEME Key Impact Pathways  
We are dealing with a real industrial need in the field of surface finishing and more specifically in the Plating on Plastics process: The elimination of use of chromic acid and palladium. Our project objectives are in full accordance with the expected outcome of our project, which is the development of new metal coating systems produced without the use of toxic substances (i.e. Cr 6+) and PGMs (i.e. Pd). It is important to state that heavy metals or other toxicants released into the environment mainly happen from the plating installations where these compounds are in a dissolved state (i.e. ions) through wastewater and sludges, rather than from the coatings, which are in a solid stable state, of the plated objects. [43] More specifically, our project’s results will contribute towards the main expected outcome and impact set by the EC as follows:  

Safe & Sustainable by Design approach  
We will develop two alternative coating solutions for PoP applications. As mentioned, the current practice is based on the use of chromic acid (Cr 6+) and Pd. The new technologies will provide the surface finishing industry with non-toxic processes, free of the use of critical raw materials (i.e. Palladium), enhancing thus the safety in the occupational environment and the environment as well as the overall resilience and sustainability of the process. Both technologies are based on REACH compliant chemicals and no listed compounds (i.e. in the list of SVCH) will be utilized. For example, the formulation of new UV-curable resins utilizing bio-based chemical platforms (i.e. isosorbide and itaconic acid) will take place instead of the use of the Bisphenol-A compound that is recognized as toxic for reproduction and an endocrine-disrupting substance that may cause adverse effects on people's health and the environment and is listed in SVCH. Additionally, mixtures of commonly used industrial acids or alkali will be used in the second path instead of chromic acid. The elimination of Cr 6+ from the PoP will be a significant innovation towards a safer and more sustainable surface finishing industry.  
Scale: The global market for chromic acid was estimated at ~600 million € during 2020, with ~8% to be used in plastic etching processes. [44] Based on the CrO3 price (i.e. 22€/kg), this translates to ~2,100 tons per year. Europe has a share of ~25%, thus, 525 tons of chromic acid are consumed in Europe annually for PoP purposes.  
Significance: The reduction of chromic acid etching process usage will have a tremendous impact on the PoP industry in terms of environmental and occupational health issues. Cr6+ has been proven to be a toxic and carcinogenic compound affecting not only the plating workers but also the citizens around the plating plants (i.e. the famous real case of Erin Brockovich against the Pacific Gas & Electric Company that operated chromic acid processes). Exposure to Cr6+ results in excess risk of lung or other types of cancers [45]. The wide adoption of SSBD technologies of our project will significantly reduce such incidents in the European PoP in the long term, targeting zero deaths after 30 years.  

New Materials Development with equal or enhanced properties compared to SoA  
The PoP is the main technology that can provide decorative and functional finishing on plastic surfaces. Practically, most PoP applications concern decorative finishes, while a small part is for engineering purposes in which properties such as hardness, wear and corrosion resistance, or EMI shielding are important. In all cases, of primary importance is the excellent adhesion of the electroless coating to the surface of the plastic. Low adhesion will lead to premature failure of the component in all cases. Thus, all studies towards the direction of novel PoP technologies focus mainly on this property. We will provide technologies that will have superior adhesion as well as other benefits.  
In the case of sprayable resins, containing Ni salts free reactive -COOH groups that act as anchoring groups to plastic surfaces, enhancing thus the adhesion of the resin to the substrate will be inserted. In the following steps, the activation of Ni sites and the electroless plating (formation of Ni-P layer) will take place. The adhesion of the latter layer is of crucial importance for the coating’s performance. Its excellent adhesion is ensured based on the distribution of the Ni nuclei that are mechanically entrapped in the resin matrix. Another benefit of the proposed technology is that PoP becomes a substrate-independent process in terms that after the deposition of the resin thin film on the substrate surface, the following steps involve the sprayed resin and not the substrate. This could be of extremely high industrial importance as it can be the basis for the metallization of various types of surfaces. On the other hand, the adhesion of the coatings prepared through the green etching process is expected to be as effective as the current chromic acid etching route, based on a double mechanism. At the micro level, mechanical interlocking of the metal film on the etched polymer surface takes place, while at the molecular level, chemical forces (i.e. Van der Waals attraction of valence bonds of metal with polar groups of plastic resulted from the etched solution) are developed.  
Scale: The global market for Plating on Plastics was estimated to be ~580 million € (2020) while the European share was ~190 million €. There is a strong increasing demand for the technology as it combines lightweight, low cost, and high aesthetic, especially in applications in the automotive and aerospace industry, where the reduction of weight has multiple beneficial impacts (i.e. reduction of fuel consumption, increased autonomy of electric cars, etc.).  
Significance: The participation of three major large enterprises in our project provides a high innovation potential for the adoption of technology in the market. Stellantis (CRF) is the sixth-largest automaker in the world, having in its portfolio brands such as Fiat, Jeep, Alfa Romeo, Opel, Citroen, Maserati, etc., being first in Europe with 854,000 cars registered during the first quarter of 2021. Arcelik is active in more than 100 countries worldwide, operating 15 production plants in Europe, Asia, and Africa, offering products under its own brand names (i.e. Beko, Grundig, Artic, etc.) or acting as a Tier 1 for other brands for hundreds of millions of spare parts. Finally, Israel Aerospace Industry operates in a high niche market, developing technologies for the aerospace industry.  

Materials Modelling  
Our activities in the modelling area are innovative, especially regarding the usage of multi-scale modelling and simulation to the overall spray application of resins for supporting the metallic plating of the plastic materials. The usage of a multi-scale approach in such multi-step processes is challenging and aligned with the goals of the European Material Modelling Council (EMMC). The need for improved linking and coupling of models to describe all relevant phenomena has already been raised by the EMMC and is addressed in our project by the proper specification of the modelling and simulation needs and the interfaces between the different scale activities. This will be reinforced by adopting the MODA (MOdelling DAta generalisation) to reach a standardized description of the required materials models and linked activities, and by also adopting the European Materials Modelling Ontology (EMMO) for our project activities definition. This will, in parallel, maximize the impact of the modelling activities beyond our project scope, by allowing future usage and linking of the developed models, potentially within the EMMC targeted European Marketplace. The usage of AI-based surrogate models to facilitate the interlinkage of models with the application allows it, is also a relevant innovation that can find applications in many other material-modelling related activities.  
Scale: The field of materials modelling spans across a wide range of disciplines, i.e. computational chemistry, continuum mechanics, thermodynamics, etc. A recent study, conducted by the EMMC-CSA and MarketPlace EC funded projects, resulted in an estimated materials modelling market size of 340 million €. [46]  
Significance: Modelling is widely used to enhance research efficiency, contributing to the reduction of costs and time by identifying alternative, more cost-effective materials and processes. Based on statistical data [47], it is estimated that the cost saving utilizing targeted materials modelling in the framework of our project (i.e. concerning the development of sprayable resins and the etching process for increasing adhesion) is ~60% of the budget allocated in WP3 and WP4, meaning ~1,000,000€ for the duration of our project.  

Market replication of new technologies  
The EC defines the meaning of market replication Innovation Actions as those aiming to support innovative advancements to reach the market after successful demonstration but not application/deployment. This is due to several obstacles that we can encounter (see next subsection). Therefore, it is often highly beneficial to make use of tools and practices that facilitate market replication, or, in other words, that help innovation make it to the market. We are designed to utilize them, aiming to maximize our impact and boost the market uptake of our results. Our consortium’s work in SSBD will produce not only sets of criteria, guides, and safe methodologies for the processes of focus, but also standardization results, via integration of SSBD findings into protocols and guidelines, useful for derivative and relevant works. Those results will essentially be combined within a bigger set of guidelines and good practices that will result from our project and will constitute one of our main outcomes. They will be widely communicated but also disseminated, targeting policymakers and stakeholders in the field, aiming for the integration of these protocols and good practices into cluster efforts, widely used by field-related stakeholders. 2.1 Expected Impacts We have presented a roadmap to support the transition to a knowledge-based post-petroleum economy in the EU by establishing sustainable and competitive bio-based industries and value chains. The Bioeconomy Strategy and its Action Plan pave the way to a more innovative, resource-efficient, and competitive EU society, while generating new incomes and ensuring job creation and accelerating progress towards the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). In this context, our project’s SIRA aims to push, among other things, the development of new biotech processes and biomaterials helping decouple economic growth from resource depletion and environmental impact. Nowadays, plastics are used in various sectors with a demand of 50 Mtons per year; plasticulture accounts for 4% (greenhouse 31%, mulch 45%, and silage 24%); while packaging makes up for 40% of this use, of which food packaging accounts for around 90%. In both sectors, the EoL treatment of plastics is underperforming. According to PlasticsEurope, in 2018, 31% of plastic waste was recycled in Europe, but landfill and incineration are still dominant treatments for plastic post-consumer waste. The recently adopted EC’s plastics strategy aims at decoupling plastics production from virgin materials, reducing their leakage into the environment, improving the economics and uptake of recycling to support the transition from a linear to a circular economy model. Our project, RECOVER, is a high-impact project for the improvement of the bioeconomy in Europe. Thus, it is hinged on delivering biotechnological tools applying microorganisms, enzymes, earthworms, and insects to degrade conventional non-recyclable plastic packaging and agricultural films waste streams but also result in new feedstocks (chitin/chitosan-based plastics and coatings and new fertilizers) for the primary sector and bio-based food industries. In this way, the circular economy concept is realized, providing added EoL for plastics which are currently landfilled or incinerated, or more often left on the soil. This impact will be achieved by performing research work with the objective of making this concept commercially viable in practice, analyzing the potentially successful exploitation pathways and assessing and managing any related risks. Importantly, our project will contribute to the achievement of the SDGs as discussed below in each relevant impact point and visualized in Fig. 2.1. As further shown in this figure, we significantly contribute to the KPIs set out in the overall BBI-JU roadmap and more specifically to the expected impacts in the topic BBI-2019-SO2-R3 Apply microorganisms and/or enzymes to resolve end-of-life issues of plastics, as described below:

EXPECTED IMPACTS LINKED TO BBI JU KPIS: 
▪ contribute to KPI 1 – create at least one new cross-sector interconnection in the bio-based economy; 

Our proposal builds upon the BBI goal of achieving the full potential of the bio-based industries, which requires swift and concerted action of today's dispersed stakeholders across various sectors, disciplines, and Member States. In RECOVER, connecting different partners, such as researchers, technology developers, and producers, through clustering and networking is key to developing new value chains and supporting the emergence of affordable EoL solutions for plastics, which also constitutes a strong contribution to the SDG 17 “Partnerships for the Goals”. The active involvement of all key players along the value chains (including additional sectorial associations in the Advisory Board) will allow us to create long-lasting links with related clusters, necessary to fill a clear gap in the marketplace for sustainable plastics and EoL thereof. With RECOVER we will establish 1 new circular cross-sector connection and further intensify 2 connections, among waste management and biotechnologies, and among the bioplastics, packaging/agriculture, and agri-food sectors as explained below. 

We will create an unprecedented systematic, cross-border and cross-sectorial, circular connection between all actors for the considered end-of-life management of non-biodegradable AWP. The new cross-sector interconnection is realized by bonding the biotechnology providers, namely the suppliers of enzymes (for the plastics degradation, represented in our consortium by ASA) and insects (for the plastics decomposition, represented by NUTRINSECT) with users (post-consumers AWP: IDELUX, ASAJA, SAV and post-industrial AWP: NATURPLAS, FEMTO) and converters (OWS RF for composting & biogas plant design, IDELUX for MSW management) of conventional fossil-based plastics. Following such an approach, we will not only allow achieving high levels of biodegradation of AWP - both from fields or landfills - but also achieving its valorization through insect by-products - such as high-value chitin-chitosan-based formulations from the conversion of AWP. These last will result in bio-fertilizers, new active mulching films, and agriculture products (pots, sticks, yarns) for the primary sector as well as in active packaging for food industries. The magnitude of our new cross-sector interconnection will be multiplied by ASAJA as a top member of COPA and SAV, whose presence in the consortium favors the generation of positive spillover effects among its affiliates leading to push-pull effects for the new RECOVER symbiotic bioprocesses. Relying on the transition from linear to circular value chains, we close the loop making farmers and food producers that generate AWP become producers of biobased, biodegradable active ingredients for bioplastics (chitin/chitosan) as well as end users of new fertilizer formulations and mulching films, pots, etc. with enhanced properties by AWP biodegradation by-products. This way we will create new upstream links with producers of unavoidable AWP opening up unprecedented business opportunities for farmers that conventionally are positioned at the end of such value chain. This opportunity reinforces the genuine urgent need of the farmers to find a viable way to handle plastic pollution in soil for their sector to be sustainable in the long term. Indeed, we will create new incomes for agri-food sectors not yet involved in waste management to actually tap into the opportunity of converting their waste into value (more details in the paragraph related to impacts associated with the creation of new incomes). As such, the current waste challenge turns into an enabler for innovation allowing a significant decrease in the environmental impact of the agri-food sectors and of the value chains of our solutions. We will have the agri-food sectors playing a strategic polyvalent end-user role within our bio-based value chain. While, as seen, they act as material suppliers along the new value chains, they need to be supplied with agricultural films as well as fertilizers (both as insect by-products, as chitin/chitosan-based formulations). Incontrovertibly, this magnifies the strength of the new links and the resonance of our solutions in the new circular bio-based value chains. 

We will further intensify 2 emerging cross-sectorial interconnections in the bio-based economy cluster: 
• We will reinforce the bond that the bioplastics sector has with the packaging sector (which is by far the largest consumer of biodegradable and bio-based plastics, expected to grow at a CAGR of 17% to 2024) by providing new demonstrators in food applications, possible thanks to our solutions’ unique EoL tailored to their needs. This new link is for example illustrated at the level of our Large packaging converter, FEMTO, and food packer MANOR. 
• We will intensify the interconnection that the bioplastics sector has with the agriculture sector (which is expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.8% to 2024) by providing new demonstrators in agriculture films, pots, sticks, etc. applications. This new bond is realized through the Agricultural Film Supplier NATURPLAS and FEMTO producer of plastic items by injection molding. Our participation in RECOVER will ensure NATURPLAS to drive the development towards actual market needs and new legislative constraints. As seen, the sectors embraced by RECOVER are of utmost significance in terms of need for new EoL with over 43% of plastic used for packaging and for agriculture, but the new cross-sectorial interdependencies created, considered also when building our AB, can contribute to the utmost exploitation of our results, also in other target sectors where plastics have difficult EoL (e.g. consumer goods, tableware, textiles, sanitary items, etc.). 

▪ contribute to KPI 2 – set the basis for at least one new bio-based value chain; 

Our consortium mobilizes the necessary partners and sectorial approach to contribute to the achievement of KPI 2 aimed at delivering “10 new bio-based value chains by 2020”. The development of a suite of novel biotech tools for the AWP biodegradation that respond to the demand for affordable and sustainable EoL solutions for plastics requires liaising a critical mass of actors that are able to generate value working in a synergistic collaboration. As such, we leverage partners at every stage of the supply and value chain, as well as additional stakeholders in the AB. In RECOVER, we create 3 new stable and effective value chains not only by new players interconnections at the upstream of the chain (to link standard plastics with insects - microorganisms and enzymes providers) but also remodeling existing value chains that will substitute their petrol-derived products with our bio-based solutions (biofertilizers, agricultural bio-films, and food packaging), as illustrated in Fig. 2.2.
